name: astrodendro

metadata.name: astrodendro
metadata.summary: Python package for computation of astronomical dendrograms

documentation:
About
-----

The aim of this module is to provide an easy way to compute dendrograms of observed or simulated Astronomical data in Python

Documentation
-------------

For information on installing and using ``astrodendro``, please visit [https://dendrograms.readthedocs.io/](https://dendrograms.readthedocs.io/)

Reporting issues
----------------

Please report issues via the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/dendrograms/astrodendro/issues)

Credits
-------

This package was developed by:

* [Braden MacDonald](https://github.com/bradenmacdonald)
* [Chris Beaumont](https://github.com/ChrisBeaumont)
* [Thomas Robitaille](https://github.com/astrofrog)
* [Adam Ginsburg](https://github.com/keflavich)
* [Erik Rosolowsky](https://github.com/low-sky)

Build and coverage status
-------------------------

[![Build Status](https://travis-ci.org/dendrograms/astrodendro.svg?branch=master)](https://travis-ci.org/dendrograms/astrodendro)
[![Coverage Status](https://coveralls.io/repos/dendrograms/astrodendro/badge.svg?branch=master)](https://coveralls.io/r/dendrograms/astrodendro?branch=master)

functions.name: astrodendro.io.util._fast_reader
documentation:
Use scipy.ndimage.find_objects to quickly identify subsets of the data
to increase speed of dendrogram loading

functions.name: astrodendro.io.util._slow_reader
documentation:
Loop over each valid pixel in the index_map and add its coordinates and
data to the flux_by_structure and indices_by_structure dicts

This is slower than _fast_reader but faster than that implementation would
be without find_objects.  The bottleneck is doing `index_map == idx` N
times.

documentation: This is a closure - we have to pass the input key to callback somehow.

methods.name: astrodendro.scatter.Scatter.update_selection
documentation: Highlight seleted structures

methods.name: astrodendro.scatter.Scatter.set_loglog
documentation: Convenience function to make the plot logarithmic

Scatter is an optional viewer that plugs into a SelectionHub.
It displays catalog properties in a scatter plot.
Users can select scatter points directly by clicking and dragging
a lasso around points of interest. These selected points'
corresponding structures will then be highlighted in all other
viewers.

Example use:

    >>> from astrodendro.scatter import Scatter
    # ... code to create a dendrogram (d) and catalog ...
    >>> dv = d.viewer()
    >>> ds = Scatter(d, dv.hub, catalog, 'radius', 'v_rms')
    >>> dv.show()

To set logarithmic scaling on the x, y axes or both, the following
convenience methods are defined:

    >>> ds.set_loglog()
    >>> ds.set_semilogx()
    >>> ds.set_semilogy()
    # These can be unset by passing a `log=False` keyword, i.e.
    >>> ds.set_loglog(False)

For more information on using Scatter, see the online
documentation.

modules.name: astrodendro.pruning
documentation:
The pruning module provides several functions to perform common
pruning via the `is_independent` keyword in the Dendrogram
{meth}`~astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.compute` method.

Examples:

```
#prune unless leaf peak value >= 5
Dendrogram.compute(data, is_independent=min_peak(5))

#prune unless leaf contains 10 pixels
Dendrogram.compute(data, is_independent=min_npix(10))

#apply both criteria
is_independent = all_true((min_peak(5), min_npix(10)))
Dendrogram.compute(data, is_independent=is_independent)
```
Licensed under an MIT open source license - see LICENSE

functions.name: astrodendro.pruning.all_true
documentation:
Combine several `is_independent` functions into one

# Parameters

functions.name: astrodendro.pruning.min_peak

functions.name: astrodendro.pruning.contains_seeds

Critieria that leaves contain at least one of a list of seed positions

Parameters

seeds : tuple of array-like

    for each seed. This is the format returned, e.g., by np.where

modules.name: astrodendro.tests._testdata
documentation:
Realistic data for unit tests and benchmarks
This module provides a 107x107x602 pixel data cube where all the flux values
greater than 1.4 represent real data from L1448 13co, but all other (lesser)
values are randomly generated.

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure._merge
documentation:
Merge a structure with the present structure.

This is a private method only intended for use by
{meth}`~astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.compute`

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.height
documentation:
This is defined as the minimum value in the children structures, or the
peak value of the present structure if it has no children.
The following methods can be used during OR after computation         #

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.ancestor
documentation:
Find the ancestor of this leaf/branch non-recursively.

This always returns an object (may return self if the object has no
parent). Results are partially cached to reduce recursion depth. The
caching assumes that once an object has been given a parent, that
parent will never change.

This method should be equivalent to:

    if self.parent == None:
        return self
    else:
        return self.parent.ancestor

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure._fill_footprint
documentation:
Set all corresponding points in `array` to the level of the structure.

# Parameters

array

: The array to write the footprint to

recursive

: Whether to also add the footprint for the sub-structures
The following methods are only reliable after the entire tree is      #
computed. They should not be used in dendrogram.py                    #

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.level
documentation:
The level of the structure, i.e. how many structures need to be traversed to reach the present structure.

This is 0 for structures in the trunk, with values increasing in steps of 1
towards the leaves.

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.newick
documentation: Newick representation of this structure.

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.descendants
documentation: Get a flattened list of all child leaves and branches.

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.get_npix
documentation:
Return the number of pixels in this structure.

Parameters
----------
subtree : bool, optional
    Whether to recursively include all sub-structures when counting the
    pixels.

Returns
-------
n_pix : int
    The number of pixels in this structure

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.get_peak
documentation:
Return (index, value) for the pixel with maximum value.

Parameters
----------
subtree : bool, optional
    Whether to recursively include all sub-structures when searching
    for the peak.

Returns
-------
index : tuple
    The n-dimensional index of the peak pixel
value : float
    The value of the peak pixel

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.sorted_leaves
documentation:
Return a list of sorted leaves.

Parameters
----------
sort_key : function, optional
    A function which given a structure will return a scalar that is
    then used for sorting. By default, this is set to a function that
    returns the peak value of a structure (including descendants).
reverse : bool, optional
    Whether to reverse the sorting.
subtree : bool, optional
    Whether to recursively include all sub-structures in the list.

Returns
-------
leaves : list
    A list of sorted leaves

methods.name: astrodendro.structure.Structure.get_mask
documentation:
Return a boolean mask outlining the structure.

# Parameters

shape

: The shape of the array upon which to compute the mask. This is only
  required if the structure is not attached to a dendrogram.

subtree

: Whether to recursively include all sub-structures in the mask.

# Returns

mask

: The mask outlining the structure (`False` values are used outside
  the structure, and `True` values inside).

A structure in the dendrogram, for example a leaf or a branch.

A structure that is part of a dendrogram knows which other structures it is
related to. For example, it is possible to get the parent structure
containing the present structure `s` by using the `parent` attribute:

```
>>> s.parent
<Structure type=branch idx=2152>
```

Likewise, the `children` attribute can be used to get a list of all
sub-structures:

```
>>> s.children
[<Structure type=branch idx=1680>, <Structure type=branch idx=5771>]
```

A number of attributes and methods are available to explore the structure
in more detail, such as the `indices` and `values` methods, which
return the indices and values of the pixels that are part of the
structure. These and other methods have a `subtree=` option, which if
`True` (the default) returns the quantities related to structure and all
sub-structures, and if `False` includes only the pixels that are part of
the structure, but excluding any sub-structure.

functions.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.periodic_neighbours
documentation:
Utility for computing neighbours on datasets with periodic boundaries.

This can be passed to the neighbours keyword of {meth}`Dendrogram.compute`

# Parameters

axes

: Which axes of the data are periodic

# Examples

Build a dendrogram where the 0th axis wraps from top-to-bottom:

```
Dendrogram.compute(data, neighbours=periodic_neighbours(0))
```

classes.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.compute
documentation:
Compute a dendrogram from a Numpy array.

# Parameters

data

: The n-dimensional array to compute the dendrogram for

min_value

: The minimum data value to go down to when computing the
  dendrogram. Values below this threshold will be ignored. Defaults
  to the minimum value in the data.

min_delta

: The minimum height a leaf has to have in order to be considered an
  independent entity.

min_npix

: The minimum number of pixels/values needed for a leaf to be considered
  an independent entity.

is_independent

: A custom function that can be specified that will determine if a
  leaf can be treated as an independent entity. The signature of the
  function should be `func(structure, index=None, value=None)`
  where `structure` is the structure under consideration, and
  `index` and `value` are optionally the pixel that is causing
  the structure to be considered for merging into/attaching to the
  tree.

  If multiple functions are provided as a list, they
  are all applied when testing for independence.

neighbours

: A function that returns the list of neighbours to a given
  location. Neighbours is called as `neighbours(dendrogram, idx)`,
  where `idx` is a tuple describing the n-dimensional location
  of a pixel. It returns a list of N-dimensional locations of
  neighbours. This function can implement optional adjacency logic.

  :::{note}
  `idx` refers to location in a copy of the input data
  that has been padded with one element along each edge.
  :::

wcs

: A WCS object that describes `data`. This is used in the
  interactive viewer to properly display the data's coordinates
  on the image axes. (Requires that `wcsaxes` is installed; see
  <http://wcsaxes.readthedocs.org/> for install instructions.)

# Examples

The following example demonstrates how to compute a dendrogram from an
dataset contained in a FITS file:

```
>>> from astropy.io import fits
>>> array = fits.getdata('observations.fits')
>>> from astrodendro import Dendrogram
>>> d = Dendrogram.compute(array)
```

# Notes

More information about the above parameters is available from the
online documentation at \[www.dendrograms.org\](www.dendrograms.org).

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.neighbours
documentation:
Return a list of indices to the neighbours of a given pixel.

This method can be overridden to handle custom layouts
(e.g., healpix maps, periodic boundaries, etc.)

Parameters
----------
idx : tuple
    The N-dimensional location of a pixel in the data

Returns
-------
List of N-dimensional locations of each neighbour

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.trunk
documentation:
A list of all structures that have no parent structure and form the
base of the tree.

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.load_from
documentation:
Load a previously computed dendrogram from a file.

# Parameters

filename

: The name of the file to load the dendrogram from. By default, the
  file format will be automatically detected from the file
  extension. At this time, only HDF5 files (extension `.hdf5`) are
  supported.

format

: The format to use to read the file. By default, this is not used
  and the format is auto-detected from the file extension. At this
  time, the only format supported is `'hdf5'`.

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.save_to
documentation:
Save the dendrogram to a file.

# Parameters

filename

: The name of the file to save the dendrogram to. By default, the
  file format will be automatically detected from the file
  extension. At this time, only HDF5 files (extension `.hdf5`) are
  supported.

format

: The format to use for the file. By default, this is not used and
  the format is auto-detected from the file extension. At this time,
  the only format supported is `'hdf5'`.

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.viewer
documentation:
Launch an interactive viewer to explore the dendrogram.

This functionality is only available for 2- or 3-d datasets.

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.prune
documentation:
Prune a dendrogram after it has been computed.

# Parameters

min_delta

: The minimum height a leaf has to have in order to be considered an
  independent entity.

min_npix

: The minimum number of pixels/values needed for a leaf to be considered
  an independent entity.

is_independent

: A custom function that can be specified that will determine if a
  leaf can be treated as an independent entity. The signature of the
  function should be `func(structure, index=None, value=None)`
  where `structure` is the structure under consideration, and
  `index` and `value` are optionally the pixel that is causing
  the structure to be considered for merging into/attaching to the
  tree.

  If multiple functions are provided as a list, they
  are all applied when testing for independence.

This class is used to compute and represent a dendrogram for a given dataset.

To create a dendrogram from an array, use the
{meth}`~astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.compute` class method:

```
>>> from astrodendro import Dendrogram
>>> d = Dendrogram.compute(array)
```

Once the dendrogram has been computed, you can explore it programmatically
using the `trunk` attribute, which allows you to access the base-level
structures in the dendrogram:

```
>>> d.trunk
[<Structure type=leaf idx=101>,
 <Structure type=branch idx=2152>,
 <Structure type=leaf idx=733>,
 <Structure type=branch idx=303>]
```

Structures can then be recursively explored. For more information on
attributes and methods available for structures, see the
{class}`~astrodendro.structure.Structure` class.

The dendrogram can also be explored using an interactive viewer. To use
this, use the {meth}`~astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.viewer` method:

```
>>> d.viewer()
```

and an interactive Matplotlib window should open.

Finally, the {meth}`~astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram.plotter` method can
be used to facilitate the creation of plots:

> ```pycon
> >>> p = d.plotter()
> ```

For more information on using the plotter and other aspects of the
{class}`~astrodendro.dendrogram.Dendrogram` class, see the online
documentation.
the formula above generalizes this special case

methods.name: astrodendro.dendrogram.TreeIndex.indices
documentation:
Return pixel indices associated with a dendrogram structure.

Returns the pixels in the original dendrogram array
which are associated with a particular structure id.

Parameters
----------
sid : integer
      The structure index to lookup. Stored in `Structure.idx`
subtree : bool, optional
      If true, return indices for subtrees as well. Default=False

Returns
-------
A tuple of integer ndarrays, akin to np.where().
This can be directly used as an index into the dendrogram
data array

classes.name: astrodendro.tests.test_recursion.TestRecursionLimit
documentation:
Test that we can efficiently compute deep dendrogram trees
without hitting the recursion limit.
Note: plot() uses recursion but we should be able to *compute*
dendrograms without using deep recursion, even if we aren't
yet able to plot them without using recursion.

modules.name: astrodendro.progressbar
documentation:
progressbar.py

A Python module with a ProgressBar class which can be used to represent a
task's progress in the form of a progress bar and it can be formated in a
basic way.

Here is some basic usage with the default options:

    >>> from progressbar import ProgressBar
    >>> p = ProgressBar()
    >>> print p
    [>............] 0%
    >>> p + 1
    >>> print p
    [=>...........] 10%
    >>> p + 9
    >>> print p
    [============>] 0%

And here another example with different options:

    >>> from progressbar import ProgressBar
    >>> custom_options = {
    ...     'end': 100,
    ...     'width': 20,
    ...     'fill': '#',
    ...     'format': '%(progress)s%% [%(fill)s%(blank)s]'
    ... }
    >>> p = ProgressBar(**custom_options)
    >>> print p
    0% [....................]
    >>> p + 5
    >>> print p
    5% [#...................]
    >>> p + 9
    >>> print p
    100% [####################]

Source: https://github.com/ikame/progressbar
Licensed under an MIT open source license - see LICENSE

classes.name: astrodendro.progressbar.ProgressBar

methods.name: astrodendro.progressbar.ProgressBar.reset
documentation: Resets the current progress to the start point

ProgressBar class holds the options of the progress bar.
The options are:
    start   State from which start the progress. For example, if start is
            5 and the end is 10, the progress of this state is 50%
    end     State in which the progress has terminated.
    width   --
    fill    String to use for "filled" used to represent the progress
    blank   String to use for "filled" used to represent remaining space.
    format  Format
    incremental

classes.name: astrodendro.progressbar.AnimatedProgressBar
documentation:
Extends ProgressBar to allow you to use it straighforward on a script.
Accepts an extra keyword argument named `stdout` (by default use sys.stdout)
and may be any file-object to which send the progress status.

documentation: In Astropy 0.3, np.sum will do the right thing for quantities, but in the mean time we need a workaround.

functions.name: astrodendro.flux.compute_flux
documentation:
Given a set of flux values in arbitrary units, find the total flux in a
specific set of units.

# Parameters

input_quantities

: A `astropy.units.quantity.Quantity` instance containing an array of
  flux values to be summed.

output_unit

: The final unit to give the total flux in (should be equivalent to Jy)

wavelength

: The wavelength of the data (required if converting e.g.
  ergs/cm^2/s/micron to Jy)

spatial_scale

: The pixel scale of the data (should be an angle)

velocity_scale

: The pixel scale of the data (should be a velocity)

beam_major

: The beam major full width at half_maximum (FWHM)

beam_minor

: The beam minor full width at half_maximum (FWHM)

modules.name: astrodendro.plot
documentation: Licensed under an MIT open source license - see LICENSE

classes.name: astrodendro.plot.DendrogramPlotter

methods.name: astrodendro.plot.DendrogramPlotter.set_custom_positions
documentation:
Manually set the positon on the structures for plotting.

# Parameters

custom_position

: This should be a function that takes a
  ``` ~astrodendro.structure.Structure`returns the position of the
  leaves to use for plotting. If the dataset has more than one
  dimension, using this may cause lines to cross. If this is used,
  then ``sort_key` ``` and `reverse` are ignored.

methods.name: astrodendro.plot.DendrogramPlotter.sort
documentation:
Sort the position of the leaves for plotting.

Parameters
----------
sort_key : function, optional
     This should be a function that takes a
     `~astrodendro.structure.Structure` and returns a scalar that is
     then used to sort the leaves. If not specified, the leaves are
     sorted according to their peak value.
reverse : bool, optional
     Whether to reverse the sorting

methods.name: astrodendro.plot.DendrogramPlotter.plot_tree
documentation:
Plot the dendrogram tree or a substructure.

# Parameters

ax

: The Axes inside which to plot the dendrogram

structure

: If specified, only plot this structure. This can be either the
  structure object itself, or the ID (`idx`) of the structure.

subtree

: If a structure is specified, by default the whole subtree will be
  plotted, but this can be disabled with this option.

autoscale

: Whether to automatically adapt the window limits to the tree

# Notes

Any additional keyword arguments are passed to
`~matplotlib.collections.LineCollection` and can be used to control the
appearance of the plot.

methods.name: astrodendro.plot.DendrogramPlotter.plot_contour
documentation:
Plot a contour outlining all pixels in the dendrogram, or a specific.
structure.

# Parameters

ax

: The Axes inside which to plot the dendrogram

structure

: If specified, only plot this structure. This can be either the
  structure object itself, or the ID (`idx`) of the structure.

subtree

: If a structure is specified, by default the whole subtree will be
  plotted, but this can be disabled with this option.

slice

: If dealing with a 3-d cube, the slice at which to plot the contour.
  If not set, the slice containing the peak of the structure will be
  shown

# Notes

Any additional keyword arguments are passed to
`~matplotlib.axes.Axes.contour` and can be used to control the
appearance of the plot.

methods.name: astrodendro.plot.DendrogramPlotter.get_lines
documentation:
Get a collection of lines to draw the dendrogram.

# Parameters

structures

: The structures to plot. If not set, the whole tree will be plotted.

subtree

: If a structure is specified, by default the whole subtree will be
  retrieved, but this can be disabled with this option.

# Returns

lines

: The lines (sub-class of LineCollection) which can be directly used in Matplotlib

# Notes

Any additional keyword arguments are passed to the
`~matplotlib.collections.LineCollection` class.
A class to plot a dendrogram object.

modules.name: astrodendro.analysis
documentation: Licensed under an MIT open source license - see LICENSE

functions.name: astrodendro.analysis._qsplit
documentation: Split a potential astropy Quantity into unit/quantity

functions.name: astrodendro.analysis._unit
documentation: Return the units associated with a number, array, unit, or Quantity

functions.name: astrodendro.analysis._make_catalog
documentation: Make a catalog from a list of structures

functions.name: astrodendro.analysis.ppv_catalog
documentation:
Iterate over a collection of position-position-velocity (PPV) structures,
extracting several quantities from each, and building a catalog.

# Parameters

structures

: The structures to catalog (e.g., a dendrogram)

metadata

: The metadata used to compute the catalog

fields

: The quantities to extract. If not provided,
  defaults to all PPV statistics

verbose

: If True (the default), will generate warnings
  about missing metadata

# Returns

table

: The resulting catalog

functions.name: astrodendro.analysis.pp_catalog
documentation:
Iterate over a collection of position-position (PP) structures, extracting
several quantities from each, and building a catalog.

# Parameters

structures

: The structures to catalog (e.g., a dendrogram)

metadata

: The metadata used to compute the catalog

fields

: The quantities to extract. If not provided,
  defaults to all PPV statistics

verbose

: If True (the default), will generate warnings
  about missing metadata

# Returns

table

: The resulting catalog

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.__init__
documentation:
Compute pixel-level statistics from a scalar field, sampled at specific
locations.

Parameters
----------
values : 1D ndarray
    data values to use
indices: tuple of 1D arrays
    Location of each element of values. The i-th array in the tuple
    describes the ith positional dimension
This class does all of the heavy computation

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.mom0
documentation: The sum of the values

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.mom1
documentation: The intensity-weighted mean position

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.mom2
documentation: The intensity-weighted covariance matrix

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.mom2_along
documentation:
Intensity-weighted variance/covariance along 1 or more directions.

Parameters
----------
direction : array like
          One or more set of direction vectors. Need not be normalized

Returns
-------
result : array
    The variance (or co-variance matrix) of the data along the
    specified direction(s).

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.paxes
documentation:
The principal axes of the data (direction of greatest elongation)

Returns
-------
result : tuple
    Ordered tuple of ndarrays

Notes
-----
Each array is a normalized direction vector. The arrays
are sorted in decreasing order of elongation of the data

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.projected_paxes
documentation:
The principal axes of a projection of the data onto a subspace

Paramters
---------
axes : array-like, (nnew, nold)
       The projection to take. Each row defines a unit vector in
       the new coordinate system

Returns
--------
result : tuple
    Tuple of arrays (nnew items)

Notes
-----
The ordered principal axes in the new space

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.ScalarStatistic.count
documentation: Number of elements in the dataset.

classes.name: astrodendro.analysis.Metadata

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.Metadata.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
key : str
       Metadata name.
description : str
       What the quantity describes
default : scalar
       Default value if metadata not provided
strict : bool
       If True, raise KeyError if metadata not provided.
       This overrides default

A descriptor to wrap around metadata dictionaries.

Lets classes reference self.x instead of self.metadata['x'],

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.SpatialBase.major_sigma
documentation:
Major axis of the projection onto the position-position (PP) plane,
computed from the intensity weighted second moment in direction of
greatest elongation in the PP plane.

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.SpatialBase.minor_sigma
documentation:
Minor axis of the projection onto the position-position (PP) plane,
computed from the intensity weighted second moment perpendicular to
the major axis in the PP plane.

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.SpatialBase.radius
documentation: Geometric mean of `major_sigma` and `minor_sigma`.

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.SpatialBase.area_ellipse
documentation:
The area of the ellipse defined by the second moments, where the
semi-major and semi-minor axes used are the HWHM (half-width at
half-maximum) derived from the moments.

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.SpatialBase.to_mpl_ellipse
documentation:
Returns a Matplotlib ellipse representing the first and second moments
of the structure.

Any keyword arguments are passed to {class}`~matplotlib.patches.Ellipse`

classes.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic.x_cen
documentation: The mean position of the structure in the x direction.

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic.y_cen
documentation: The mean position of the structure in the y direction.

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic.v_cen
documentation:
The mean velocity of the structure (where the velocity axis can be
specified by the `vaxis` metadata parameter, which defaults to 0
following the Numpy convention - the third axis in the FITS convention).

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic.flux
documentation:
The integrated flux of the structure, in Jy (note that this does not
include any kind of background subtraction, and is just a plain sum of
the values in the structure, converted to Jy).

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic.v_rms
documentation:
Intensity-weighted second moment of velocity (where the velocity axis
can be specified by the `vaxis` metadata parameter, which defaults to
0 following the Numpy convention - the third axis in the FITS
convention).

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic.position_angle
astrodendro.analysis.PPVStatistic.area_exact
documentation: The exact area of the structure on the sky.

Compute properties of structures in a position-position-velocity (PPV)
cube.

# Parameters

structure

: The structure to compute the statistics for

metadata

: Key-value pairs of metadata

classes.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPStatistic

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPStatistic.flux
documentation:
The integrated flux of the structure, in Jy (note that this does not
include any kind of background subtraction, and is just a plain sum of
the values in the structure, converted to Jy).

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPStatistic.position_angle
documentation:
The position angle of sky_maj, sky_min in degrees counter-clockwise
from the +x axis.

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPStatistic.x_cen
documentation:
The mean position of the structure in the x direction (in pixel
coordinates, or in world coordinates if the WCS transformation is
available in the meta-data).

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPStatistic.y_cen
documentation:
The mean position of the structure in the y direction (in pixel
coordinates, or in world coordinates if the WCS transformation is
available in the meta-data).

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPStatistic.area_exact
documentation: The exact area of the structure on the sky.

Compute properties of structures in a position-position (PP) cube.

# Parameters

structure

: The structure to compute the statistics for

metadata

: Key-value pairs of metadata

methods.name: astrodendro.analysis.PPPStatistic.__init__
documentation:
Derive properties from PPP density and velocity fields.

This is not currently implemented

Parameters
----------
rhostat : ScalarStatistic instance
vstat : VectorStatistic instance
